primary_diagnosis.name: hEDS visceral (gastroparesis) flare with significant dehydration and urinary retention — surgical abdomen to be excluded

pathophysiology_summary: hEDS commonly involves autonomic and connective-tissue dysfunction affecting the viscera: gastroparesis/dysmotility causes intractable nausea, non-bilious vomiting and bloating, while dysautonomia produces orthostatic hypotension and tachycardia, and pelvic-floor/autonomic dysfunction can cause urinary retention. A severe flare with 24 hours of vomiting produces significant dehydration approaching hypovolaemic shock (postural BP 100/60→85/50, HR to 135), inability to rehydrate orally, and a palpable retained bladder. This mimics mechanical bowel obstruction and DKA. Reassuring features are non-bilious/non-faeculent vomiting with passage of flatus and a soft non-peritonitic abdomen, and a normal glucose without Kussmaul breathing. However, telehealth cannot examine the abdomen/bladder, measure BP, give IV fluids/antiemesis or catheterise. The correct action is urgent same-day in-person/ED assessment for IV rehydration, IV antiemesis and bladder decompression, and to exclude a surgical abdomen — not remote antiemetics or small-sip oral rehydration — with 000 for green/bloody vomiting, a rigid abdomen, or collapse.

differential.evidence_basis: Intractable non-bilious vomiting with dysautonomia on known hEDS.

differential.diagnosis: hEDS visceral flare with dehydration and urinary retention
differential.evidence_basis: Non-bilious vomiting, flatus passed, soft abdomen, normal glucose — a visceral/autonomic flare, but with shock-level dehydration and retention.
Lower probability but requires in-person confirmation.
The active concern requiring IV rehydration.

differential.diagnosis: hEDS visceral flare with dehydration and urinary retention
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth position: visceral/autonomic flare with shock-level dehydration and retention needing urgent in-person/ED IV fluids, antiemesis, bladder decompression, and surgical-abdomen exclusion.

red_flags.description: Orthostatic hypotension/tachycardia with inability to keep fluids down (impending hypovolaemic shock)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Severe volume depletion risks collapse and AKI; oral rehydration is inadequate.

red_flags.description: Urinary retention (unable to empty; palpable bladder)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Retention needs decompression (catheter) to prevent bladder/renal injury.

red_flags.description: Green/bilious or bloody vomiting, or a rigid board-like abdomen (surgical abdomen)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate obstruction/perforation — a surgical emergency.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Advising remote antiemetics/small sips for a patient with orthostatic hypotension, inability to rehydrate orally and urinary retention.
Attributing everything to hEDS and missing a surgical abdomen or the retention needing a catheter.
Arranging IM prochlorperazine over chat instead of IV therapy and assessment.
Not screening glucose/ketones/breathing in a vomiting patient.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Orthostatic hypotension (85/50 standing) with tachycardia and dry mucosa — significant dehydration approaching shock
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports urgent in-person/ED IV rehydration and assessment, plus bladder decompression.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Abdominal and bladder examination and postural vitals
IV rehydration, IV antiemesis and bladder catheterisation
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Screen surgical abdomen/DKA and recognise shock-level dehydration + retention; arrange urgent same-day in-person/ED assessment for IV fluids, IV antiemesis and bladder decompression; escalate to 000 for green/bloody vomiting, a rigid abdomen, or collapse.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Managing remotely with antiemetics/small sips could miss a surgical abdomen and leave a shocked, retaining patient to collapse.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Intractable vomiting with dehydration requires in-person assessment, IV rehydration/antiemesis, and exclusion of a surgical cause.
Acute urinary retention requires prompt bladder decompression (catheterisation) and assessment of the cause.
